g.data-narration: Welcome to Optimal Binary Search Trees - a dynamic programming solution for minimizing expected search costs.
An Optimal Binary Search Tree minimizes average search cost using known access probabilities for each key.
g.text: What is an Optimal Binary Search Tree?
A BST that minimizes expected search cost based on known probabilities of searching for each key.
Unlike regular BSTs, OBST considers how often each key is accessed to optimize performance.

g.data-narration: With keys 10, 20, 30 and search probabilities shown, placing key 20 at root gives cost 1.30 vs 1.90.
We define p_i as successful search probability for key k_i, and q_i as unsuccessful search probability between keys.
g.text: p_i: Probability of successful search for key k_i (i = 1 to n)
q_i: Probability of unsuccessful search between k_i and k_i+1
q_0: less than k_1 | q_n: greater than k_n | Dummy keys d_0 to d_n

g.data-narration: Expected cost equals sum of each key probability times depth plus one, plus dummy key probabilities times their depths.
g.text: Cost = Sum p_i times (depth(k_i) + 1) + Sum q_i times (depth(d_i) + 1)
Depth of root = 0. Each level deeper adds one comparison.

g.data-narration: OBST has optimal substructure: if k_r is root, left subtree must be optimal for keys k_1 to k_{r-1}, right for k_{r+1} to k_n.
The DP recurrence: c[i][j] equals w[i][j] plus minimum of c[i][r-1] plus c[r][j] for all possible roots r.
g.text: Recurrence: c[i][j] = w[i][j] + min_r { c[i][r-1] + c[r][j] }

g.data-narration: Let us work through an example with 4 keys. The probabilities are given in these tables - notice they sum to 1.0.
Initialize: c[i][i] = q_i and w[i][i] = q_i for all i from 0 to n. This represents empty subtrees.
For length-1 chains (one key), compute w[0][1], w[1][2], w[2][3], w[3][4] and find optimal roots.
For single keys, c[i][j] equals w[i][j] since both child subtrees are empty with cost q.
g.text: For chains of length 1, there is only one possible root:

g.data-narration: For length-2 chains, we try each key as root and pick the one minimizing w plus children costs.
After computing all subproblems, c[0][4] gives minimum expected cost. The root table builds the optimal tree structure.
Basic algorithm runs in O(n cubed) time due to three nested loops. Knuth optimization reduces it to O(n squared).
Remember these key formulas: c[i][i] equals q_i, w recurrence, and the main recurrence for c[i][j].
For exams: practice the worked example, memorize the recurrence, verify probabilities sum to 1, and know how to reconstruct the tree.
g.text: 1. Practice the worked example step by step
2. Memorize the recurrence relation c[i][j] = w[i][j] + min{...}
3. Do not forget to add w[i][j] in the recurrence
5. Know tree reconstruction from root table
